retrieved_docs.title: Von Economo Encephalitis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Von Economo ruled out external sources such as sausage poisoning and gas poison. As there was no contact with another individual, meningitis, and polio were also ruled out. Discussions related to the etiology of influenza were still mentioned. However, his cases and a few other reports concluded that the encephalitis lethargica is a separate disorder than directly caused by the influenza infection. Still, influenza as a prodromal phase was not ruled out by von Economo. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Edema -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Through these mechanisms, cerebral edema stems from tumor, trauma, hypoxia, infection, metabolic derangements, or acute hypertension.  Causes are widespread and divide into neurological and non-neurological categories.  Other non-neurologic causes include hepatitis, Reye syndrome, carbon monoxide poisoning, lead poisoning, and high altitude cerebral edema. A rare cause of cerebral edema is pseudotumor cerebri.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Necrotizing Encephalomyelopathy -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the high mortality and rarity of SNE, mothers, and fathers of affected individuals must be screened for any genetic abnormalities. In addition, the risk of having additional children should be discussed with each family. Once the disease has manifested, it is imperative to diagnose the disease subtype, since this can affect both the course and efficacy of treatment. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Carbon Monoxide Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The presenting signs and symptoms can be vague and nonspecific; therefore, one needs to have a high index of suspicion to avoid misdiagnosis. The incidence of CO poisoning is higher during cold weather months when there is also a higher incidence of viral gastroenteritis infections, and many of the symptoms can overlap. Common symptoms of CO toxicity are malaise, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and headache. More severe cases can result in impaired cognitive function, loss of consciousness, seizures, and coma. It is important to suspect a loss of consciousness if the patient is unable to give a history of recent events. The classic “cherry red” lips and skin appearance associated with CO poisoning is insensitive and often not present. Cardiac toxicity can present with chest pain, myocardial ischemia, ventricular arrhythmia, acute congestive heart failure, and often profound metabolic lactic acidosis. Anyone with smoke inhalation, unexplained loss of consciousness, or coma should have the diagnosis of CO poisoning entertained. If the patient is conscious, it is helpful to test cognitive function with a mini-mental status exam. Dysdiadochokinesis (difficulty with rapid alternating movements) is impaired with CO poisoning and is a quick means of assessing cerebellar function. It is reasonable to assume that if the patient has cerebellar dysfunction from CO poisoning, the rest of the brain function will probably be impaired.

retrieved_docs.title: Lead Encephalopathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In first-world nations, focusing on eliminating lead in gasoline and household paints has significantly decreased the incidence of high-dose lead exposures. However, low-dose chronic exposures, as well as higher-dose exposures in industrial settings and developing nations, continue to be an issue. One of the most deadly late-stage complications of lead intoxication is lead encephalopathy. [3] Mediated primarily by lead’s effects on cerebrovascular endothelium, lead encephalopathy often presents in vague or subtle ways that other effects of lead toxicity may also complicate. While uncommon, lead encephalopathy is a life-threatening illness that must be considered, diagnosed, and treated in the appropriate clinical settings. [3]

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the pathophysiology of radiation-induced brain necrosis. Describe the presentation of a patient with radiation-induced brain necrosis. Explain the importance of improving care coordination amongst interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by radiation-induced brain necrosis. retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of radiation necrosis of the brain ranges from 2.5 to 24% of irradiated patients. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: SCD due to nitrous oxide abuse is increasingly recognized. This has resulted from an exponential increase in recreational nitrous oxide (Whippits) use over the past decade. It is now the second most common recreational drug use among 16-24-year-olds in the UK. [15] About 3.4% of nitrous oxide users experience neurological symptoms consistent with SCD. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: High-Pressure Neurological Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: High pressure poses the greatest challenge to human physiology during deep diving. Factors such as the high concentration of gases, increased matter density, and alterations in the typical properties of heat and sound can also lead to neurological signs independent of HPNS. [8] Conditions associated with deep diving include oxygen toxicity, exposure to polluted breathing gases, nitrogen narcosis, HPNS, decompression illness, and carbon dioxide retention due to increased gas density. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Progressive Supranuclear Palsy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cause of progressive supranuclear palsy is unknown. Advanced age [5] and environmental factors such as exposure to toxins are theorized causes. [6] [7] The tau protein aggregates may be due to an unconventional infectious agent, random genetic mutations, or some unknown chemical in the food, air, or water which slowly damages certain vulnerable areas of the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: Neurogenic Pulmonary Edema -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The underlying causes of NPE reported in the literature include the following common and rarer etiologies: Common etiologies Acute subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) Intracerebral haemorrhage Traumatic brain injury (eg, subdural/epidural hematoma, brainstem or upper cervical spinal trauma) Epilepsy Rare etiologies Enterovirus 71-associated brainstem encephalitis Ischemic stroke Intracranial/spinal surgery Multiple sclerosis Electroconvulsive therapy Meningitis Poisoning Drug-induced (pemetrexed) [4] Hypoxia Aqueductal glioma Spinal cord infarction [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1588
retrieved_docs.content: Other and unspecified environmental and accidental causes is classified as a child category under the broader parent grouping “e codes: other specified; nec,” indicating that this specific set of environmental and accidental etiologies is organized within the external cause (E‑code) taxonomy as an “other specified; not elsewhere classified” subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2088
retrieved_docs.content: The condition described as “Injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft” is linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). In turn, the tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة) is associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity). This relationship indicates that injuries resulting from an aircraft crash, even when the intent is unclear, may involve underlying tissue damage that can give rise to neurotoxic effects, highlighting the importance of evaluating both structural and neurotoxic consequences in the clinical assessment and management of such trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2015
retrieved_docs.content: Coma, stupor, and brain damage are clinical manifestations that frequently co‑occur with عَوَز الأكسجين, indicating that insufficient oxygen supply to the brain can lead to these severe neurological states.

retrieved_docs.content: Asphyxiation and strangulation are medical events that are classified under the category of other injuries and conditions due to external causes, indicating that they arise from external mechanisms rather than internal disease processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 207
retrieved_docs.content: are organized within injury classification frameworks, share common external cause codes, and often involve similar pathological outcomes such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity, providing a cohesive framework for assessment and management of traumatic patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2562
retrieved_docs.content: Injury due to legal intervention by firearm and injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted by crashing of aircraft are both linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة is itself associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that such traumatic events may involve neurotoxic processes alongside structural tissue injury. Recognizing the connection between these mechanisms can inform clinical assessment and management of patients with firearm‑related legal interventions or aircraft‑related trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 833

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide (oxid uhelnatý) is classified as a gasotransmitter, a small gaseous signaling molecule involved in physiological processes. In addition to its signaling role, carbon monoxide is closely associated with carbon monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a toxic condition that occurs when inhaled concentrations are high enough to impair oxygen delivery and cause cellular hypoxia. Understanding carbon monoxide’s dual nature as both a biologically active gasotransmitter and a potential toxin is important for clinical assessment, prevention of exposure, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Overige hersenaandoeningen, which translates to “other brain disorders,” are closely linked to إصابة في الدماغ (brain injury). Within the spectrum of brain injuries, تمزق خيمي (chemical injury) is recognized as a specific subtype, indicating that chemical injuries are encompassed by the broader category of brain injury. This relationship highlights that other brain disorders often involve various forms of brain injury, including those caused by chemical mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2200
retrieved_docs.content: Neurotoxicity (سمية عصبية) is closely linked with tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة), which represents the characteristic morphological change seen in neurotoxic injury. The presence of tissue damage serves as a key indicator of neurotoxic effects on the nervous system, aiding clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and managing conditions where neurotoxic agents cause structural injury to tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide, referred to in Czech as oxid uhelnatý, is classified both as a gasotransmitter and as an antimetabolite. As a gasotransmitter, it functions as a small, endogenously produced gaseous signaling molecule that can modulate vascular tone, neurotransmission, and inflammatory responses. Its antimetabolite properties allow it to interfere with normal metabolic pathways, for example by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration. Exposure to carbon monoxide is directly associated with carbon‑monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a clinical syndrome characterized by hypoxic injury, neurological deficits, and potentially fatal outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual role as a physiological messenger and a toxic agent is essential for accurate diagnosis, prevention, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is identified as a specific manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that brain injuries occurring during the birthing process are encompassed within the broader category of birth trauma. Recognizing this relationship highlights the importance of evaluating neurological complications when assessing and managing birth‑related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2308
retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the presence of diffuse encephalitis around the axis, as indicated by الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, suggesting that these conditions may involve or utilize this widespread inflammatory process in the brain.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.
